title: Family and domestic violence leave

meta.description: Paid family and domestic violence leave entitlements for all employees including casuals.

headings.text: Who is entitled to family and domestic violence leave?

content:
All employees are entitled to paid family and domestic violence leave under the NES. This includes full-time, part-time, and casual employees.

From 1 August 2023, all employees covered by the national workplace relations system are entitled to 10 days of paid family and domestic violence leave each year.

This leave applies from the first day of employment — employees don't need to accumulate it over time.

What is family and domestic violence?
Family and domestic violence means violent, threatening or other abusive behaviour by a close relative of the employee, a member of the employee's household, or a current or former intimate partner, that seeks to coerce or control the employee or causes them harm or fear.

When can it be taken?
An employee can take family and domestic violence leave when they are experiencing family and domestic violence and they need to do something to deal with the impact of the violence, and it is impractical to do this outside of their ordinary hours of work.

Examples of circumstances include:
- Making arrangements for their safety or the safety of a close relative
- Attending court hearings
- Accessing police services
- Attending medical, counselling or financial services appointments
- Arranging alternative accommodation

Notice and evidence:
An employee must let their employer know as soon as possible that they need to take the leave. This can happen after the leave has started. The employer can ask for evidence to confirm the leave is taken for a valid reason. Evidence could include:
- Documents from the police
- Court documents
- Statutory declaration
- Documents from a medical professional, domestic violence support service, lawyer or financial adviser

The type of evidence the employer asks for must be reasonable. It does not have to be a formal document.

Pay rate:
Employees are paid their full pay rate for the hours they would have worked during the leave period. For casual employees, this is based on the hours they were rostered to work.

Confidentiality:
Employers must take all reasonable steps to ensure information about an employee taking family and domestic violence leave is kept confidential. Only disclose information if required by law or with the employee's consent.

Pay slips:
Pay slips must NOT mention that an employee has taken family and domestic violence leave. This is a specific legal requirement to protect the employee's safety and privacy.

Support services:
- 1800RESPECT: 1800 737 732 (national domestic, family and sexual violence counselling service)
- Police: 000 (immediate danger)
tables: From day 1 of employment (no accumulation required)
Yes — 10 days per year (does not accumulate if unused)
As soon as possible (can be after leave starts)
Must NOT appear on pay slip
